Benchmark Results for gemini-2.5-flash-HighReasoning-Tools

Overall Summary

Test 1: Travelling Salesman Problem Solver (C++) Score: 5.00 / 21
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) in C++.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY number of cities from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 10-40 cities (brute-force feasible for smallest cases)
- **Medium**: 100-1000 cities (requires heuristics)
- **Large**: 5000-100000 cities (requires efficient heuristics)
- **Extreme**: 500000-5000000 cities (requires very fast approximations)
- **Epic**: 10000000+ cities (requires highly optimized approximations)

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with city counts ranging from 10 to 10000000. The same algorithm must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N (number of cities)
Lines 2..N+1: x y coordinates (floats)

**Output format (stdout):**
One line with N integers: the visit order of city indices (0-indexed), starting at city 0.
Do NOT include returning to city 0 (return is implicit).

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on problem size
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 300 seconds even for 1000,000,000 cities
3. **Quality**: Should produce reasonable routes at all scales
4. **Correctness**: Output must be a valid permutation of 0..N-1 starting with 0

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35730 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

Write complete, compilable C++ code with a main() function that reads stdin and writes stdout.
Include adaptive logic that chooses different strategies based on N.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: 10 Cities
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Given a list of cities, find the shortest round-trip route that visits every city exactly once and returns to the start. This is one of the most famous hard problems in computer science — checking every possible route is only feasible for a handful of cities, so the AI must use clever shortcuts to find a good route quickly.

Subpasses scale from 10 cities up to 1 000, so brute-force is impossible for the larger cases. The AI's route length is compared against a simple baseline; beating it earns full marks.

 1.0000
[10 cities] Route distance: 2449.07, Baseline: 2922.93, Ratio: 0.84x, Time: 0.0s - excellent (within 10% of baseline)

TSP Solver - 10 Cities

View Code (5457 chars)
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <cmath>
#include <algorithm>
#include <numeric>
#include <limits> // For numeric_limits

// Structure to represent a city
struct Point {
    float x, y;
    int id; // Original index of the city
};

// Function to calculate Euclidean distance between two points
double distance(const Point& p1, const Point& p2) {
    return std::hypot(p1.x - p2.x, p1.y - p2.y);
}

// Placeholder for Nearest Neighbor algorithm
std::vector<int> nearest_neighbor(int N, const std::vector<Point>& cities) {
    std::vector<int> tour;
    tour.reserve(N);
    std::vector<bool> visited(N, false);

    int current_city_idx = 0; // Start at city 0
    tour.push_back(cities[current_city_idx].id);
    visited[current_city_idx] = true;

    for (int i = 1; i < N; ++i) {
        double min_dist = std::numeric_limits<double>::max();
        int next_city_idx = -1;

        for (int j = 0; j < N; ++j) {
            if (!visited[j]) {
                double dist = distance(cities[current_city_idx], cities[j]);
                if (dist < min_dist) {
                    min_dist = dist;
                    next_city_idx = j;
                }
            }
        }
        tour.push_back(cities[next_city_idx].id);
        visited[next_city_idx] = true;
        current_city_idx = next_city_idx;
    }
    return tour;
}

// Helper function to calculate the total length of a tour
double calculate_tour_length(int N, const std::vector<Point>& cities, const std::vector<int>& tour) {
    double total_dist = 0.0;
    for (int i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
        total_dist += distance(cities[tour[i]], cities[tour[(i + 1) % N]]);
    }
    return total_dist;
}

// 2-Opt local search
std::vector<int> two_opt(int N, const std::vector<Point>& cities, std::vector<int> current_tour) {
    std::vector<int> best_tour = current_tour;
    double best_dist = calculate_tour_length(N, cities, best_tour);

    bool improved = true;
    while (improved) {
        improved = false;
        for (int i = 0; i < N - 1; ++i) { // First edge: (tour[i], tour[i+1])
            for (int k = i + 2; k < N; ++k) { // Second edge: (tour[k], tour[k+1])
                                            // Ensure k is not adjacent to i. k+1 will wrap around for last city.

                // Get the actual point objects using their IDs
                // (tour[i], tour[i+1]) and (tour[k], tour[k+1]) are the edges to swap
                Point p_i = cities[best_tour[i]];
                Point p_i_plus_1 = cities[best_tour[(i + 1) % N]];
                Point p_k = cities[best_tour[k]];
                Point p_k_plus_1 = cities[best_tour[(k + 1) % N]];

                // Calculate old segment distances
                double old_segment_dist = distance(p_i, p_i_plus_1) + distance(p_k, p_k_plus_1);

                // Calculate new segment distances after reversal
                // The new edges are (tour[i], tour[k]) and (tour[i+1], tour[k+1])
                double new_segment_dist = distance(p_i, p_k) + distance(p_i_plus_1, p_k_plus_1);

                if (new_segment_dist < old_segment_dist) {
                    std::reverse(best_tour.begin() + i + 1, best_tour.begin() + k + 1);
                    best_dist = calculate_tour_length(N, cities, best_tour); // Recalculate full length
                    improved = true;
                    // Restart search from beginning to allow further improvements
                    i = 0; // Reset i to re-scan for improvements
                    break; // Break inner loop
                }
            }
        }
    }
    return best_tour;
}


// Placeholder for Angle-Sweep algorithm
std::vector<int> angle_sweep(int N, const std::vector<Point>& cities) {
    if (N <= 1) return {0};
    if (N == 2) return {0, 1};

    // We use city 0 as the reference point for angles
    Point ref_point = cities[0];

    // Create a vector of pairs: {angle, original_index} for cities other than city 0
    std::vector<std::pair<double, int>> angled_cities;
    for (int i = 1; i < N; ++i) {
        double angle = std::atan2(cities[i].y - ref_point.y, cities[i].x - ref_point.x);
        angled_cities.push_back({angle, cities[i].id});
    }

    // Sort cities based on their angle
    std::sort(angled_cities.begin(), angled_cities.end());

    std::vector<int> tour;
    tour.reserve(N);
    tour.push_back(ref_point.id); // Start with city 0

    for (const auto& ac : angled_cities) {
        tour.push_back(ac.second); // Add sorted cities to the tour
    }

    return tour;
}


int main() {
    // Fast I/O
    std::ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    std::cin.tie(NULL);

    int N;
    std::cin >> N;

    std::vector<Point> cities(N);
    for (int i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
        std::cin >> cities[i].x >> cities[i].y;
        cities[i].id = i;
    }

    // Handle trivial cases
    if (N == 0) {
        return 0; // No cities, no output
    }
    if (N == 1) {
        std::cout << "0" << std::endl;
        return 0;
    }

    std::vector<int> tour;

    if (N <= 1000) { // Small to Medium scale: Nearest Neighbor + 2-Opt
        tour = nearest_neighbor(N, cities);
        tour = two_opt(N, cities, tour);
    } else { // Large, Extreme, Epic scale: Angle-Sweep Algorithm
        tour = angle_sweep(N, cities);
    }

    // Print the tour
    for (int i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
        std::cout << tour[i] << (i == N - 1 ? "" : " ");
    }
    std::cout << std::endl;

    return 0;
}

Test 2: Chinese Postman Problem Solver (C#) Score: 0.00 / 13
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the Chinese Postman Problem (Route Inspection Problem) in C#.

You must write a C# solver that can handle ANY graph size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 6-20 nodes, 8-45 edges (small graphs, exact algorithms feasible)
- **Medium**: 35-200 nodes, 80-600 edges (requires efficient algorithms)
- **Large**: 500-1000 nodes, 2000-5000 edges (requires optimized implementations)
- **Extreme**: 5000-10000 nodes, 30000-80000 edges (requires very fast algorithms)
- **Epic**: 50000-100000 nodes, 500000-1000000 edges (requires highly optimized algorithms)

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with graphs ranging from 6 nodes to 100000 nodes. The same function must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N M (number of nodes, number of edges)
Lines 2..M+1: u v w (edge endpoints, 0-indexed, weight integer)

**Output format (stdout):**
A sequence of node indices (whitespace-separated) representing a route that starts at node 0,
traverses EVERY edge at least once, and returns to node 0.
You may optionally prefix with a single integer L (route length). If present, it must match the number of nodes listed.

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on graph size and complexity
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for large graphs
3. **Correctness**: Must traverse every edge at least once

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

Imagine a mail carrier who must walk along every street in a neighbourhood and return home. Some streets may need to be walked twice if the network doesn't allow a perfect loop — the goal is to minimise that extra walking.

The AI must figure out which streets (edges) to re-traverse and produce a complete route. Subpasses increase the graph size, making the problem harder to solve efficiently.

Chinese Postman Problem - 6 nodes, 8 edges

View Code (617 chars)
The C# solver for the Chinese Postman Problem has been successfully implemented, compiled, and tested. The solution correctly identifies odd-degree vertices, calculates shortest paths between them using Dijkstra's algorithm, finds a minimum weight perfect matching using a Bitmask DP approach, and constructs an Eulerian circuit using Hierholzer's algorithm. The code successfully processed sample input for a non-Eulerian graph and produced the expected route. The solution addresses the critical requirements of scalability and correctness.

The generated C# code is located in `ChinesePostmanSolverApp/Program.cs`.

Test 3: Graph Layout - Edge Crossing Minimization (Rust) Score: 0.00 / 16
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a Graph Layout problem (Edge Crossing Minimization) in Rust.

You must write a Rust solver that can handle ANY graph size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 6-15 nodes, small trees and cycles (planar, 0 crossings achievable)
- **Medium**: 20-100 nodes, dense planar and non-planar graphs
- **Large**: 500-1000 nodes, random dense graphs (many crossings unavoidable)
- **Extreme**: 5000-10000 nodes, very large random graphs

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with graphs ranging from 6 nodes to 200,000 nodes. The same solver must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N M (number of nodes, number of edges)
Lines 2..M+1: u v (undirected edge endpoints, 0-indexed)

**Output format (stdout):**
Output N lines, one per node, with: x y coordinates (floats).
You may optionally prefix with a single integer N on the first line.

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on graph size and density
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for large graphs
3. **Quality**: Should produce reasonable layouts with minimal crossings
4. **Robustness**: Handle disconnected components, self-loops, and parallel edges gracefully

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Given a graph (a set of dots connected by lines), arrange the dots on a 2D canvas so that as few lines cross each other as possible. A clean, readable layout is the goal — tangled spaghetti is the enemy.

Minimising crossings is extremely hard in general. The AI must place nodes intelligently; the baseline simply puts them in a circle. Subpasses increase the number of nodes and edges.

View Code (10922 chars)
use std::io::{self, BufRead, Write};
use std::f64;
use rand::Rng; // For random initial positions
use std::time::Instant; // For performance measurement

// Struct to represent a node with its position and displacement
struct Node {
    pos: [f64; 2],
    disp: [0.0; 2],
}

fn main() -> io::Result<()> {
    let start_time = Instant::now();
    let stdin = io::stdin();
    let mut lines = stdin.lock().lines();

    // Read N and M (number of nodes and edges)
    let first_line = lines.next().ok_or_else(|| io::Error::new(io::ErrorKind::UnexpectedEof, "Empty input"))??;
    let nm: Vec<usize> = first_line
        .split_whitespace()
        .filter_map(|s| s.parse().ok())
        .collect();

    if nm.len() != 2 {
        return Err(io::Error::new(io::ErrorKind::InvalidInput, "Expected two integers (N M) on the first line"));
    }
    let n = nm[0];
    let m = nm[1];

    if n == 0 { // Handle empty graph
        let stdout = io::stdout();
        let mut handle = stdout.lock();
        writeln!(handle, "0")?; // Output N = 0
        return Ok(());
    }

    // Adjacency list representation of the graph
    let mut adj: Vec<Vec<usize>> = vec![vec![]; n];

    // Read edges
    for i in 0..m {
        let line = lines.next().ok_or_else(|| io::Error::new(io::ErrorKind::UnexpectedEof, format!("Missing edge line {}", i + 2)))??;
        let edge: Vec<usize> = line
            .split_whitespace()
            .filter_map(|s| s.parse().ok())
            .collect();

        if edge.len() != 2 {
            return Err(io::Error::new(io::ErrorKind::InvalidInput, format!("Expected two integers (u v) for edge on line {}", i + 2)));
        }
        let u = edge[0];
        let v = edge[1];

        // Validate node indices
        if u >= n || v >= n {
            return Err(io::Error::new(io::ErrorKind::InvalidInput, format!("Node index {} or {} out of bounds (0-{}) on line {}", u, v, n - 1, i + 2)));
        }

        adj[u].push(v);
        adj[v].push(u); // Graph is undirected
    }

    // Initialize nodes with random positions and zero displacement
    let mut nodes: Vec<Node> = Vec::with_capacity(n);
    let mut rng = rand::thread_rng();

    // Define the bounding box for initial placement and clamping
    let min_coord = 0.0;
    let max_coord = 1000.0; // Default canvas size

    for _i in 0..n {
        nodes.push(Node {
            pos: [rng.gen_range(min_coord..max_coord), rng.gen_range(min_coord..max_coord)],
            disp: [0.0, 0.0],
        });
    }

    // Graph layout parameters (constants for Fruchterman-Reingold)
    let area = (max_coord - min_coord) * (max_coord - min_coord);
    let k = (area / n as f64).sqrt(); // Optimal distance between nodes
    let c_rep = k * k; // Repulsive constant (often k^2)
    let c_attr = k;    // Attractive constant (often k)

    // Adaptive number of iterations based on graph size
    // For smaller graphs, more iterations yield better quality.
    // For larger graphs, fewer iterations are necessary for performance.
    let max_iterations = match n {
        0..=15 => 100,      // Trivial
        16..=100 => 70,     // Medium
        101..=500 => 50,    // Large-ish
        501..=5000 => 30,   // Larger graphs
        _ => 15,            // Extreme (5000+ nodes): very few iterations to meet time limits
    };
    let mut temperature = 100.0; // Initial "temperature" or step size
    let cooling_factor = 0.95; // Rate at which temperature decreases each iteration

    // Determine grid size for repulsive force optimization
    // Grid dimension scales with N's square root, capped to reasonable values.
    // This creates a spatial hash to quickly find nearby nodes.
    let grid_dim_f64 = (n as f64).sqrt().max(2.0).min(200.0);
    let grid_dim = grid_dim_f64 as usize;
    let cell_size = (max_coord - min_coord) / grid_dim_f64;

    // --- Force-Directed Layout Loop ---
    for _iter in 0..max_iterations {
        // Check if elapsed time is too long for extreme graphs
        if n > 5000 && start_time.elapsed().as_secs_f64() > 25.0 {
            break; // Stop early to meet performance requirements
        }

        // Reset displacements for all nodes at the beginning of each iteration
        for node in &mut nodes {
            node.disp = [0.0; 2]; // Use array literal for clarity and direct assignment
        }

        // --- Repulsive forces calculation (optimized using a grid) ---
        // Populate the grid: each cell contains a list of node indices
        // that fall within its spatial bounds.
        let mut grid: Vec<Vec<Vec<usize>>> = vec![vec![vec![]; grid_dim]; grid_dim];
        for i in 0..n {
            let p_i = nodes[i].pos;
            // Calculate grid coordinates for node i, relative to min_coord
            let grid_x = ((p_i[0] - min_coord) / cell_size).floor() as usize;
            let grid_y = ((p_i[1] - min_coord) / cell_size).floor() as usize;

            // Clamp grid coordinates to ensure they are always within the grid bounds
            let grid_x = grid_x.min(grid_dim - 1).max(0);
            let grid_y = grid_y.min(grid_dim - 1).max(0);
            grid[grid_x][grid_y].push(i);
        }

        // Calculate repulsive forces for each node by looking at its neighbors in the grid
        for i in 0..n {
            let p_i = nodes[i].pos;
            let grid_x = ((p_i[0] - min_coord) / cell_size).floor() as usize;
            let grid_y = ((p_i[1] - min_coord) / cell_size).floor() as usize;
            let grid_x = grid_x.min(grid_dim - 1).max(0);
            let grid_y = grid_y.min(grid_dim - 1).max(0);

            // Iterate over the current cell and its 8 surrounding neighbor cells (3x3 area)
            for dx in -1..=1 {
                for dy in -1..=1 {
                    let neighbor_grid_x_isize = grid_x as isize + dx;
                    let neighbor_grid_y_isize = grid_y as isize + dy;

                    // Check if the neighbor cell coordinates are valid (within grid bounds)
                    if neighbor_grid_x_isize >= 0 && neighbor_grid_x_isize < grid_dim as isize &&
                       neighbor_grid_y_isize >= 0 && neighbor_grid_y_isize < grid_dim as isize {

                        let neighbor_grid_x = neighbor_grid_x_isize as usize;
                        let neighbor_grid_y = neighbor_grid_y_isize as usize;

                        // Iterate through all nodes found in the valid neighbor cell
                        for &j in &grid[neighbor_grid_x][neighbor_grid_y] {
                            if i == j { continue; } // A node does not exert repulsive force on itself

                            let p_j = nodes[j].pos;
                            let delta = [p_j[0] - p_i[0], p_j[1] - p_i[1]];
                            let distance_sq = delta[0].powi(2) + delta[1].powi(2);
                            let distance = distance_sq.sqrt();

                            if distance == 0.0 {
                                // If two nodes are at the exact same position, apply a small random displacement
                                // to prevent division by zero and help them separate.
                                nodes[i].disp[0] -= rng.gen_range(-1.0..1.0);
                                nodes[i].disp[1] -= rng.gen_range(-1.0..1.0);
                            } else {
                                // Apply Fruchterman-Reingold repulsive force: F_rep = k^2 / d
                                let force = c_rep / distance;
                                nodes[i].disp[0] -= (delta[0] / distance) * force;
                                nodes[i].disp[1] -= (delta[1] / distance) * force;
                            }
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }

        // --- Attractive forces calculation (O(M)) ---
        // Iterate through all edges and apply attractive forces between connected nodes.
        for u in 0..n {
            for &v in &adj[u] {
                // To avoid processing each edge twice (e.g., u-v and v-u),
                // and to prevent self-loops from affecting attractive forces.
                if u < v {
                    let p_u = nodes[u].pos;
                    let p_v = nodes[v].pos;

                    let delta = [p_v[0] - p_u[0], p_v[1] - p_u[1]];
                    let distance_sq = delta[0].powi(2) + delta[1].powi(2);
                    let distance = distance_sq.sqrt();

                    if distance != 0.0 {
                        // Apply Fruchterman-Reingold attractive force: F_attr = d^2 / k
                        let force = distance_sq / c_attr;
                        let disp_x = (delta[0] / distance) * force;
                        let disp_y = (delta[1] / distance) * force;

                        nodes[u].disp[0] += disp_x;
                        nodes[u].disp[1] += disp_y;
                        nodes[v].disp[0] -= disp_x; // Equal and opposite force on the other node
                        nodes[v].disp[1] -= disp_y;
                    }
                }
            }
        }

        // --- Update node positions and apply temperature ---
        for i in 0..n {
            let d_i = nodes[i].disp;

            let displacement_magnitude_sq = d_i[0].powi(2) + d_i[1].powi(2);
            let displacement_magnitude = displacement_magnitude_sq.sqrt();

            if displacement_magnitude > temperature {
                // If the displacement is too large, scale it down by the current temperature
                // This prevents nodes from moving too much in one step, ensuring stability.
                let scale = temperature / displacement_magnitude;
                nodes[i].pos[0] += d_i[0] * scale;
                nodes[i].pos[1] += d_i[1] * scale;
            } else {
                // Otherwise, apply the full calculated displacement
                nodes[i].pos[0] += d_i[0];
                nodes[i].pos[1] += d_i[1];
            }

            // Clamp node positions to the defined bounding box.
            // This prevents nodes from moving outside the drawing area and stabilizes the layout.
            nodes[i].pos[0] = nodes[i].pos[0].max(min_coord).min(max_coord);
            nodes[i].pos[1] = nodes[i].pos[1].max(min_coord).min(max_coord);
        }

        // Decrease the temperature. The layout "cools down", and node movements become smaller,
        // allowing the graph to settle into a stable configuration.
        temperature *= cooling_factor;
    }

    // --- Output Results ---
    let stdout = io::stdout();
    let mut handle = stdout.lock();
    // Output N on the first line, as specified by the problem (optional prefix).
    writeln!(handle, "{}", n)?;

    // Output the final x, y coordinates for each node, formatted to 6 decimal places.
    for node in nodes {
        writeln!(handle, "{:.6} {:.6}", node.pos[0], node.pos[1])?;
    }

    Ok(())
}

Test 4: Regular Tetrahedron Packing in Polyhedra (C++) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a 3D Packing problem: Pack regular tetrahedrons into polyhedra (C++).

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY container size from trivial to ludicrous scale.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: V edge_length
Lines 2..V+1: x y z (container vertices)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: K (number of tetrahedrons placed)
Lines 2..K+1: cx cy cz rx ry rz (center + Euler angles in radians)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on container volume and complexity
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for massive containers
3. **Quality**: Maximize number of tetrahedrons while ensuring no overlaps

**Key Geometry (edge length e=1.0):**
- Height: h = e * sqrt(2/3) ≈ 0.8165
- Circumradius: R = e * sqrt(3/8) ≈ 0.6124
- Inradius: r = e / sqrt(24) ≈ 0.2041

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35730 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

Write complete, compilable C++ code with a main() function.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)

Pack as many identical triangular pyramids (tetrahedra) as possible inside a given 3D shape without any overlaps. Unlike cubes, tetrahedra cannot tile space perfectly, so clever placement and rotation are essential.

The AI must handle 3D collision detection and geometry. Subpasses use increasingly complex container shapes. The baseline uses a simple grid placement strategy.

Test 5: Hamiltonian Path on Grid with Obstacles (Rust) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a Hamiltonian Path problem on 2D grids with obstacles in Rust.

You must write a Rust solver that can handle ANY grid size from trivial to ludicrous scale.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: width height obstacle_count require_cycle(0/1)
Next obstacle_count lines: x y (blocked cells)

**Output format (stdout):**
Either:
- Line 1: 0 (no path exists)
OR
- Line 1: L (path length)
- Next L lines: x y coordinates of the path in order

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on grid size and obstacle density
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for 2500 cell grids
3. **Correctness**: Must find valid paths or correctly report impossibility

**Constraints:**
- Path must start at (0, 0)
- Path must visit every non-obstacle cell exactly once
- Movement is only to adjacent cells (up/down/left/right)
- If require_cycle is True, last cell must be adjacent to (0, 0)

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Write complete, compilable Rust code with a main() function.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0

View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Find a path on a grid that visits every open cell exactly once, moving only up/down/left/right. Some cells are blocked by obstacles. Starting from the top-left corner, the AI must thread a single continuous path through every remaining cell — no revisits allowed.

This is an NP-complete problem, so brute-force search blows up quickly. Subpasses increase the grid size and obstacle count. The baseline uses a simple depth-first search.

--> C:\Users\Ash\AppData\Local\Temp\tmpusvoywxw.rs:9:7

9 | 1. **Warnsdorff's Rule:** This heuristic guides the search by prioritizing mo

Hamiltonian Path - 4x4 grid, 1 obstacles

View Code (4010 chars)
The problem requires solving the Hamiltonian Path problem on a 2D grid with obstacles, starting at (0,0), visiting all non-obstacle cells exactly once, and optionally forming a cycle back to (0,0). The critical requirements include scalability, performance (within 5 minutes for 2500 cell grids), and correctness.

**Approach Taken:**
I implemented a recursive backtracking algorithm. This is a common approach for Hamiltonian path problems.

**Optimizations Applied:**
To enhance performance and scalability, the following key optimizations were integrated:

1.  **Warnsdorff's Rule:** This heuristic guides the search by prioritizing moves to adjacent unvisited cells that have the fewest available moves (i.e., fewest unvisited neighbors). This strategy aims to keep more options open for longer, thereby reducing the likelihood of getting trapped in a dead end prematurely. The neighbors are sorted based on this rule, exploring the most constrained paths first.

2.  **Connectivity Pruning:** At each step of the recursive search, a crucial pruning step was added. Before exploring a new move, a Breadth-First Search (BFS) is performed on the *remaining unvisited, non-obstacle cells*. If these cells do not form a single connected component, it means that no Hamiltonian path can be formed from the current state, and the algorithm immediately backtracks. This prevents the exploration of entire branches of the search space that are guaranteed to fail.

3.  **Optimized BFS for Connectivity Check:** The internal BFS within the connectivity check was optimized by switching from `std::collections::HashSet` (which has hashing overhead) to a `Vec<Vec<bool>>` (direct array access) for tracking visited cells during the BFS. This provides a constant-factor speedup for grid-based visited tracking.

4.  **Early Exit for Isolated Cells:** A further optimization was added to the `check_connectivity` function. If the very first unvisited, non-obstacle cell found (to start the BFS) has no unvisited, non-obstacle neighbors, and there are still more cells to visit, it indicates that this cell is isolated from the rest of the graph. In such cases, the `check_connectivity` function can immediately return `false` without performing a full BFS, thus quickly pruning disconnected paths.

**Reason for Failure to Meet Performance Requirement:**
Despite implementing these common and effective optimizations for backtracking algorithms, the solution consistently timed out for the benchmark's larger grid sizes (e.g., 2500 cells) within the 5-minute limit.

The Hamiltonian Path problem is NP-hard. For "ludicrous scale" inputs (like a 50x50 grid with 2500 cells), the search space for a general backtracking algorithm, even with aggressive pruning, remains astronomically large. While the implemented optimizations significantly reduce the number of branches explored, they do not change the fundamental exponential time complexity in the worst case.

Meeting the demanding performance requirement for such large inputs typically necessitates a different class of algorithms:
*   **Highly specialized grid algorithms:** These exploit specific topological properties of grids, but efficient polynomial-time algorithms for general Hamiltonian paths on grids are not known.
*   **Problem transformation to SAT/Exact Cover:** Converting the problem to a Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT) or an Exact Cover problem and leveraging highly optimized external solvers (like MiniSat or Dancing Links) can solve very large instances. However, implementing such a transformation and solver from scratch, or integrating external libraries, was outside the immediate scope and constraints of this task.

In conclusion, the implemented solution is correct for finding Hamiltonian paths within a backtracking paradigm and incorporates robust pruning, but the inherent NP-hardness of the problem and the strict performance targets for "ludicrous scale" inputs exceed the practical capabilities of this algorithmic approach.

Test 6: Orbital TSP - Space Station Route Planning (C#) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving an Orbital Travelling Salesman Problem in C#.

You must write a C# solver that can handle ANY number of stations from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 2-5 stations (simple brute-force permutations feasible)
- **Medium**: 6-10 stations (requires heuristics, some optimization)
- **Large**: 15-20 stations (complex orbital mechanics, needs efficient algorithms)
- **Extreme**: 50-100 stations (very complex, requires advanced heuristics)

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with 2 to 100 stations. The same algorithm must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N mu
Line 2: start orbit: x y z vx vy vz
Lines 3..N+2: station orbits: x y z vx vy vz

**Output format (stdout):**
One line containing N integers: the visitation order of station indices (0..N-1).


**Starting position (epoch T=0):**
Position: (7197.9, 3733.4, -6152.3) km
Velocity: (4.567, -4.577, -1.601) km/s
Altitude: ~3807 km

**Physics constants:**
- Earth gravitational parameter μ = 398600.4418 km³/s²
- Earth radius = 6371 km

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on number of stations
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for 100 stations
3. **Accuracy**: Should provide reasonable delta-V estimates

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C# Compiler:**
C# (csc.exe 5.5.0-2.26208.13 (3dc781932f83b0792b0d1717a68db3f9604a2cb1)) | max LangVersion 14

Write complete, compilable C# code with a static void Main method.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Visit a series of space stations orbiting Earth using the least fuel possible, then return to the starting station. Unlike a normal route-planning problem, every target is moving at thousands of metres per second and the “cost” of travelling between two stations depends on orbital physics, not straight-line distance.

The AI must combine route optimisation with orbital mechanics. Subpasses add more stations and trickier orbit geometries. The baseline uses a simple nearest-neighbour heuristic with approximate fuel estimates.

 0.0000
[2 stations] Compilation failed:
C:\Users\Ash\AppData\Local\Temp\tmpy004nub6.cs(1,6): error CS1040: Preprocessor directives must appear as the first non-whitespace character on a line
C:\Users\Ash\AppData\Local\Te

Orbital TSP - 2 stations

View Code (1792 chars)
The C# solver is implemented in `Program.cs`.

**Key Features:**
- **Hybrid TSP Solver:** For a small number of stations (N <= 10), it uses a brute-force approach to find the optimal permutation. For larger numbers of stations, it employs a greedy Nearest Neighbor heuristic followed by a 2-Opt local search improvement, balancing accuracy and performance.
- **Simplified Orbital Mechanics:** The `CalculateTransferCost` function provides "reasonable delta-V estimates" by approximating transfers based on changes in semi-major axis (energy) and inclination. This simplified model prioritizes computational efficiency required for large N (up to 100 stations) over a full, iterative Lambert solver. Penalties are included for position and velocity mismatch to encourage rendezvous.
- **Robust Input Handling:** The `Program` class handles input parsing and constructs the cost matrix for the TSP solver.
- **Output Format:** The program outputs a single line of N integers representing the visitation order of stations (0 to N-1), as specified.

**Code Structure:**
- `Vector3D` struct for double-precision 3D vector operations.
- `OrbitalState` struct to store position and velocity vectors.
- `OrbitalMechanics` static class containing the `CalculateTransferCost` method.
- `TSPSolver` class with methods for brute-force, Nearest Neighbor, 2-Opt, permutation generation, and path cost calculation.
- `Program` class for main execution logic, input/output, and orchestrating the TSP solution.

The solution successfully compiles without warnings and runs with provided example input, producing the expected output format. The heuristic orbital mechanics and TSP algorithms are designed to meet the performance and scalability requirements for up to 100 stations within the given time limits.

Typical Prompt:
You are implementing a CSG (Constructive Solid Geometry) Union operation in C++.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY mesh complexity from trivial (6 vertex unit cubes) to
ludicrous scale (millions of vertices). The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: VA FA VB FB (vertex/face counts for mesh A and B)
Next VA lines: x y z (mesh A vertices)
Next FA lines: a b c (mesh A faces, 0-indexed)
Next VB lines: x y z (mesh B vertices)
Next FB lines: a b c (mesh B faces)

You can assume that the input geometry is clean, manifold, watertight, and free of self-intersections.

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: V F (vertex and face counts for union mesh)
Next V lines: x y z
Next F lines: a b c (0-indexed triangle indices)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on mesh complexity
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for very complex meshes
3. **Correctness**: Must output correct union geometry. Must be watertight, connected,
manifold, free of holes and self intersections..

I have developed a C++ solver for the CSG Union operation using the Manifold library.

Here's a summary of the work done:
1.  **Researched CSG Libraries:** Identified the Manifold library as the most suitable for the benchmark's requirements of scalability, performance, and correctness for large meshes.
2.  **Cloned Manifold Library:** Cloned the Manifold GitHub repository to examine its license and build instructions. Confirmed the Apache 2.0 License.
3.  **Investigated Build Process:** Reviewed `README.md` and `CMakeLists.txt` for Manifold's build process, noting compatibility with MSVC and the recommendation for static linking on Windows.
4.  **Developed `solver.cpp`:** Wrote the C++ code for `solver.cpp` which:
    *   Reads mesh A and mesh B data from `stdin` into `manifold::MeshGL` structures.
    *   Creates `manifold::Manifold` objects from the input data.
    *   Performs the Union operation using `operator+`.
    *   Extracts the resulting mesh data using `GetMeshGL()`.
    *   Outputs the vertex and face data of the union mesh to `stdout`.
5.  **Provided Build and Usage Instructions:** Created a `README.md` file that comprehensively details:
    *   Prerequisites (CMake, MSVC, Git).
    *   Step-by-step instructions for cloning and building the Manifold library with appropriate CMake flags for the specified environment (MSVC, C++20, static linking, parallelization).
    *   Instructions for compiling `solver.cpp` using `cl.exe`, including how to specify include and library paths.
    *   The expected input and output formats.
    *   An example of how to run the compiled solver.

The `solver.cpp` and `README.md` files are now available in the workspace. I am confident that this solution meets all the critical requirements of the benchmark prompt.

Typical Prompt:
You are writing a maze solver in Rust that must handle ANY maze complexity from trivial to ludicrous scale.

Your program will be tested with mazes ranging from 5x5 to 50000x50000.
The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Maze format:**
- 'A' = Start position
- 'B' = Goal/End position
- '#' = Wall (cannot pass through)
- ' ' (space) = Open path (can walk through)

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: H W (height and width)
Next H lines: the maze row strings (each W characters)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: N (number of steps in path)
Next N lines: x y (0-indexed coordinates from top-left)
Path should include both start (A) and end (B) positions.
Movement is only up/down/left/right (no diagonals).
Output nothing (or 0) if no path exists.

**Constraints:**
- Path must be continuous (each step adjacent to previous)
- Path must not go through walls ('#')
- Path must start at 'A' and end at 'B'
- Path must not loop even if the maze allows it.
- If multiple solutions exist, return any one.

Architecture: AMD64

Test 9: 1D Cutting Stock - Minimum Waste (C#) Score: 10.50 / 15
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the 1D Cutting Stock Problem in C#.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N stock_length
Line 2: N cut lengths separated by spaces

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: num_stocks
Next num_stocks lines: space-separated cut indices assigned to that stock (0-indexed)

**Example:**
For cuts=[30, 40, 50] and stock_length=100:
Input:
3 100
30 40 50

Output:
2
0 1
2

**Constraints:**
- Each cut must come from a single stock piece (no gluing)
- Multiple cuts can come from the same stock piece if they fit
- No cut can exceed stock_length
- Must handle varying numbers of cuts efficiently. You can use
multiple threads, you can micro-optimise code, you can write clever algorithms,
whatever it takes to handle the problem and solve it within 5 minutes even if
there are millions of cuts.

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3

Write complete, compilable C# code with a static void Main method.

Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Given fixed-length stock material and a shopping list of smaller pieces to cut, use as few stock pieces as possible. Every requested piece must be cut from somewhere, but any leftover material is wasted — so packing the cuts tightly matters.

This is essentially a bin-packing problem and is NP-hard. Subpasses scale from a handful of cuts up to millions, forcing the AI to write code that is both correct and fast. The baseline uses a simple first-fit-decreasing heuristic.

 1.0000
[5 cuts, stock=100] Stocks used: 2, Waste: 20, Time: 0.1s - (waste < 1 stock, no repack check)

Cutting Stock - 5 cuts, stock=100

View Code (2307 chars)
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;

public class Cut
{
    public int Length { get; set; }
    public int OriginalIndex { get; set; }
}

public class Stock
{
    public int RemainingCapacity { get; set; }
    public List<int> CutIndices { get; set; }

    public Stock(int stockLength)
    {
        RemainingCapacity = stockLength;
        CutIndices = new List<int>();
    }

    public void AddCut(int cutLength, int originalIndex)
    {
        RemainingCapacity -= cutLength;
        CutIndices.Add(originalIndex);
    }
}

public class Solution
{
    public static void Main(string[] args)
    {
        // Read N and stock_length
        string[] line1 = Console.ReadLine()!.Split(' ');
        int N = int.Parse(line1[0]);
        int stock_length = int.Parse(line1[1]);

        // Read cut lengths
        string[] line2 = Console.ReadLine()!.Split(' ');
        List<Cut> cuts = new List<Cut>(N);
        for (int i = 0; i < N; i++)
        {
            cuts.Add(new Cut { Length = int.Parse(line2[i]), OriginalIndex = i });
        }

        // Sort cuts in descending order of length for FFD
        // This is crucial for the First Fit Decreasing heuristic
        cuts.Sort((a, b) => b.Length.CompareTo(a.Length));

        List<Stock> stocks = new List<Stock>();

        foreach (var cut in cuts)
        {
            bool placed = false;
            // Iterate through existing stocks to find the first one that can fit the cut
            foreach (var stock in stocks)
            {
                if (stock.RemainingCapacity >= cut.Length)
                {
                    stock.AddCut(cut.Length, cut.OriginalIndex);
                    placed = true;
                    break; // Found a fit, move to the next cut
                }
            }

            if (!placed)
            {
                // No existing stock could fit the cut, create a new one
                Stock newStock = new Stock(stock_length);
                newStock.AddCut(cut.Length, cut.OriginalIndex);
                stocks.Add(newStock);
            }
        }

        // Output results
        Console.WriteLine(stocks.Count);
        foreach (var stock in stocks)
        {
            Console.WriteLine(string.Join(" ", stock.CutIndices));
        }
    }
}
Cut Plan Visualization (5 cuts, stock=100)

Colored segments are cuts (labels show lengths when there is room). Red segment is waste.

Test 10: 2D Cutting Stock - Rectangle Packing (C++) Score: 11.70 / 13
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the 2D Cutting Stock Problem (Rectangle Bin Packing) in C++.

Given the option to purchase fixed size large 2D boards, and a list of rectangles needed,
minimize the number of boards purchased by cleverly planning your cuts and minimising waste.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY problem size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 4-8 rectangles, 100x100-200x150 boards
- **Ludicrous**: 500-50000 rectangles, 2000x2000-10000x10000 boards

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N board_width board_height
Next N lines: w h (rectangle dimensions)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: num_boards
Next N lines: board_index x y rotated (one per rectangle, rotated is 0 or 1)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on problem size and board dimensions
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for 50000 rectangles
3. **Quality**: Minimize number of boards used while producing valid placements

**Constraints:**
- Rectangles may be rotated 90° if it helps
- No overlapping allowed
- Rectangles must not exceed board boundaries

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35730 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Pack a set of rectangles into as few fixed-size boards as possible, with no overlapping. Rectangles may be rotated 90° to fit better. Think of it like fitting as many labels as you can onto sheets of card stock.

This 2D packing problem is NP-hard. Subpasses increase the number and variety of rectangles. The baseline stacks rectangles in simple horizontal rows.

 1.0000
[4 rectangles, 100x100 board] Boards: 1, Baseline: 1, Waste: 4100, Time: 0.0s - excellent (≤ baseline). (waste < 1 board, no repack check)

2D Cutting Stock - 4 rectangles, 100x100 board

Algorithm: My apologies, but I am unable to compile the provided C++ code. The Microsoft Visual C++ (MSVC) compiler (`cl.exe`) is not found in the system's PATH, and attempts to set up the environment using common `vcvarsall.bat` or `vsdevcmd.bat` paths have failed. Due to restrictions that prevent me from searching the entire file system or requesting user input for specific installation paths, I cannot properly configure the compiler environment. Despite this environmental limitation, I have completed t...

View Code (10842 chars)
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>
#include <numeric> // For std::iota
#include <limits>  // For std::numeric_limits

// Forward declaration for Rectangle struct
struct Rectangle;

// Represents an available space on a board
struct FreeRectangle {
    int x, y, width, height;

    // Helper to check if two rectangles intersect
    bool Intersects(const FreeRectangle& other) const {
        return x < other.x + other.width && x + width > other.x &&
               y < other.y + other.height && y + height > other.y;
    }

    // Helper to check if this FreeRectangle contains another
    bool Contains(const FreeRectangle& other) const {
        return x <= other.x && y <= other.y &&
               x + width >= other.x + other.width &&
               y + height >= other.y + other.height;
    }
};

// Represents an input rectangle
struct Rectangle {
    int id;             // Original index for output
    int width;
    int height;
    int original_width; // Stores original dimensions for rotation logic
    int original_height;
    int board_idx = -1; // Index of the board it's placed on
    int x = -1;         // X-coordinate of placement
    int y = -1;         // Y-coordinate of placement
    bool rotated = false; // True if rotated 90 degrees

    // Constructor
    Rectangle(int id, int w, int h) : id(id), width(w), height(h), original_width(w), original_height(h) {}

    // Helper to get area for sorting
    long long area() const {
        return static_cast<long long>(width) * height;
    }
};

// Helper function to subtract a rectangle 'B' from rectangle 'A'.
// Returns a list of new rectangles that represent the area of 'A' not covered by 'B'.
std::vector<FreeRectangle> SubtractRect(const FreeRectangle& A, const FreeRectangle& B) {
    std::vector<FreeRectangle> result;

    // If A and B don't intersect, A is unchanged
    if (!A.Intersects(B)) {
        result.push_back(A);
        return result;
    }

    // A list of candidate rectangles that cover the original 'A'
    // This creates up to 4 rectangles around B's intersection with A.
    // We effectively carve B out of A.

    // Top slice of A, above B
    if (A.y < B.y) {
        result.push_back({A.x, A.y, A.width, B.y - A.y});
    }
    // Bottom slice of A, below B
    if (A.y + A.height > B.y + B.height) {
        result.push_back({A.x, B.y + B.height, A.width, (A.y + A.height) - (B.y + B.height)});
    }

    // Left slice of A, to the left of B, bounded by B's vertical extent
    if (A.x < B.x) {
        int sub_y = std::max(A.y, B.y);
        int sub_height = std::min(A.y + A.height, B.y + B.height) - sub_y;
        if (sub_height > 0) {
            result.push_back({A.x, sub_y, B.x - A.x, sub_height});
        }
    }
    // Right slice of A, to the right of B, bounded by B's vertical extent
    if (A.x + A.width > B.x + B.width) {
        int sub_y = std::max(A.y, B.y);
        int sub_height = std::min(A.y + A.height, B.y + B.height) - sub_y;
        if (sub_height > 0) {
            result.push_back({B.x + B.width, sub_y, (A.x + A.width) - (B.x + B.width), sub_height});
        }
    }

    // Filter out invalid rectangles (width or height <= 0)
    std::vector<FreeRectangle> filtered_result;
    for (const auto& fr : result) {
        if (fr.width > 0 && fr.height > 0) {
            filtered_result.push_back(fr);
        }
    }
    return filtered_result;
}


// Manages the packing for a single board
struct Board {
    int id;
    int board_width;
    int board_height;
    std::vector<FreeRectangle> free_rects; // List of available spaces on this board

    Board(int id, int width, int height) : id(id), board_width(width), board_height(height) {
        free_rects.push_back({0, 0, width, height});
    }

    // Method to place a rectangle on this board
    bool PlaceRectangle(Rectangle& rect);

private:
    // Helper methods for MaxRects
    void SplitFreeRectangle(const Rectangle& placed_rect);
    void MergeFreeRectangles();
    // BSSF heuristic: returns the index of the best free rectangle
    int FindBestFit(int rect_width, int rect_height, int& out_x, int& out_y, bool& out_rotated);
};

// Implementation of Board methods

// Finds the best free rectangle to place the given rect_width, rect_height
// using Best Short Side Fit (BSSF) heuristic.
// Returns the index of the best free rectangle, or -1 if no fit.
int Board::FindBestFit(int rect_width, int rect_height, int& out_x, int& out_y, bool& out_rotated) {
    int best_free_rect_idx = -1;
    int best_score = std::numeric_limits<int>::max(); // BSSF score: min(remaining_short_side)

    // Try original orientation
    for (size_t i = 0; i < free_rects.size(); ++i) {
        const auto& free_rect = free_rects[i];
        if (rect_width <= free_rect.width && rect_height <= free_rect.height) {
            // Calculate remaining space after placement
            int remaining_width = free_rect.width - rect_width;
            int remaining_height = free_rect.height - rect_height;
            int score = std::min(remaining_width, remaining_height);

            if (score < best_score) {
                best_score = score;
                best_free_rect_idx = i;
                out_x = free_rect.x;
                out_y = free_rect.y;
                out_rotated = false;
            }
        }
    }

    // Try rotated orientation
    int rotated_rect_width = rect_height;
    int rotated_rect_height = rect_width;
    for (size_t i = 0; i < free_rects.size(); ++i) {
        const auto& free_rect = free_rects[i];
        if (rotated_rect_width <= free_rect.width && rotated_rect_height <= free_rect.height) {
            // Calculate remaining space after placement
            int remaining_width = free_rect.width - rotated_rect_width;
            int remaining_height = free_rect.height - rotated_rect_height;
            int score = std::min(remaining_width, remaining_height);

            // If this rotated fit is better than the current best (or if no best yet)
            // and it's not the same score as an unrotated fit that already won
            if (score < best_score) {
                best_score = score;
                best_free_rect_idx = i;
                out_x = free_rect.x;
                out_y = free_rect.y;
                out_rotated = true;
            }
        }
    }

    return best_free_rect_idx;
}

// Splits free rectangles based on the newly placed rectangle.
void Board::SplitFreeRectangle(const Rectangle& placed_rect) {
    std::vector<FreeRectangle> new_free_rects_temp;
    FreeRectangle placed_area = {placed_rect.x, placed_rect.y, placed_rect.width, placed_rect.height};

    for (const auto& free_rect : free_rects) {
        // If the free_rect does not intersect the placed_area, keep it as is.
        if (!free_rect.Intersects(placed_area)) {
            new_free_rects_temp.push_back(free_rect);
        } else {
            // If it intersects, subtract the placed_area from the free_rect.
            // This will produce new free rectangles (0 to 4) representing the remaining space.
            std::vector<FreeRectangle> subtracted_parts = SubtractRect(free_rect, placed_area);
            new_free_rects_temp.insert(new_free_rects_temp.end(), subtracted_parts.begin(), subtracted_parts.end());
        }
    }
    free_rects = new_free_rects_temp;
}

// Merges free rectangles to reduce redundancy.
void Board::MergeFreeRectangles() {
    // Sort free rectangles to help with merging.
    // Sort by x, then y, then width, then height.
    std::sort(free_rects.begin(), free_rects.end(), [](const FreeRectangle& a, const FreeRectangle& b) {
        if (a.x != b.x) return a.x < b.x;
        if (a.y != b.y) return a.y < b.y;
        if (a.width != b.width) return a.width < b.width;
        return a.height < b.height;
    });

    std::vector<FreeRectangle> merged_list;
    for (const auto& current_fr : free_rects) {
        bool contained = false;
        for (const auto& existing_fr : merged_list) {
            if (existing_fr.Contains(current_fr)) {
                contained = true;
                break;
            }
        }
        if (!contained) {
            merged_list.push_back(current_fr);
        }
    }
    free_rects = merged_list;
}

// Attempts to place a rectangle on this board.
bool Board::PlaceRectangle(Rectangle& rect) {
    int best_x, best_y;
    bool best_rotated;

    int current_rect_width = rect.width;
    int current_rect_height = rect.height;

    int best_free_rect_idx = FindBestFit(current_rect_width, current_rect_height, best_x, best_y, best_rotated);

    if (best_free_rect_idx != -1) {
        rect.x = best_x;
        rect.y = best_y;
        rect.rotated = best_rotated;
        rect.board_idx = this->id;

        if (best_rotated) {
            std::swap(rect.width, rect.height);
        }

        // Update free rectangles
        SplitFreeRectangle(rect);
        MergeFreeRectangles(); // Merge after splitting to clean up

        return true;
    }
    return false;
}

int main() {
    // Optimize C++ standard streams for competitive programming.
    // Unties cin from cout and prevents synchronization with C's stdio.
    std::ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    std::cin.tie(NULL);

    int N, board_width, board_height;
    std::cin >> N >> board_width >> board_height;

    std::vector<Rectangle> rectangles;
    rectangles.reserve(N);
    for (int i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
        int w, h;
        std::cin >> w >> h;
        rectangles.emplace_back(i, w, h);
    }

    // Sort rectangles by decreasing area (BFD heuristic)
    std::sort(rectangles.begin(), rectangles.end(), [](const Rectangle& a, const Rectangle& b) {
        return a.area() > b.area();
    });

    std::vector<Board> boards;
    boards.reserve(N / 2); // Heuristic initial reservation

    for (auto& rect : rectangles) {
        bool placed = false;
        // Try to place on existing boards
        for (auto& board : boards) {
            if (board.PlaceRectangle(rect)) {
                placed = true;
                break;
            }
        }

        // If not placed, create a new board
        if (!placed) {
            boards.emplace_back(boards.size(), board_width, board_height);
            // Place on the newly created board
            boards.back().PlaceRectangle(rect); // This should always succeed
        }
    }

    // Output results
    std::cout << boards.size() << std::endl;

    // Sort rectangles back to original ID order for output
    std::sort(rectangles.begin(), rectangles.end(), [](const Rectangle& a, const Rectangle& b) {
        return a.id < b.id;
    });

    for (const auto& rect : rectangles) {
        std::cout << rect.board_idx << " " << rect.x << " " << rect.y << " " << (rect.rotated ? 1 : 0) << std::endl;
    }

    return 0;
}

Test 11: 2D Polygon Cutting Stock (Rust) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a 2D Polygon Cutting Stock Problem in Rust.

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with problems ranging from 2 simple pieces to 5000+ complex polygons.
The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: SV NP (number of stock polygon vertices, number of pieces)
Next SV lines: x y (stock polygon vertices)
For each piece:
Line: PV (number of vertices in this piece)
Next PV lines: x y (piece polygon vertices)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: num_stocks
Next NP lines: stock_index x y rotation (one per piece)
stock_index: 0-indexed stock polygon
x y: translation to apply
rotation: angle in radians

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on number and complexity of pieces
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for 5000+ complex pieces
3. **Quality**: Minimize stock usage while ensuring valid placements

**Constraints:**
- Each placed piece must fit entirely within the stock polygon
- Pieces on same stock must not overlap
- Pieces may be rotated to any angle

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Write complete, compilable Rust code with a main() function.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Cut a set of irregular polygon shapes out of stock sheets with as little waste as possible. Unlike rectangle packing, the pieces can be any shape — stars, L-shapes, curves — so fitting them together is much harder.

The AI must handle polygon geometry (containment, intersection, rotation) and plan efficient layouts. Subpasses increase the number and complexity of the pieces. The baseline uses a simple greedy placement strategy.

Test 12: 3D Bin Packing - Polyhedra in Box (C#) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a 3D Bin Packing problem in C#.

You must write a C# solver that can handle ANY problem size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: Small containers (5x5x5), simple polyhedra
- **Medium**: Medium containers (10x10x10-20x20x20)
- **Large**: Large containers (50x50x50-100x100x100)
- **Extreme**: Massive containers (200x200x200-500x500x500)

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: V F (vertex/face count of the polyhedron)
Next V lines: x y z (polyhedron vertices)
Next F lines: a b c (polyhedron face indices)
Last line: W H D (container width, height, depth)
Container origin at (0,0,0), extends to (W,H,D).

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: count (number of polyhedra packed)
Next count lines: tx ty tz qw qx qy qz
tx,ty,tz: translation
qw,qx,qy,qz: rotation quaternion (w is scalar part)

**Quaternion Rotation Reference:**
- Identity (no rotation): 1 0 0 0
- 90° around Z: 0.707 0 0 0.707
- To rotate a point: p' = q * p * q^(-1)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on container size and polyhedron complexity
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for massive containers
3. **Quality**: Maximize packing count while ensuring valid placements

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C# Compiler:**
C# (csc.exe 5.5.0-2.26208.13 (3dc781932f83b0792b0d1717a68db3f9604a2cb1)) | max LangVersion 14

Write complete, compilable C# code with a static void Main method.
Include adaptive logic that chooses different strategies based on problem scale.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Pack as many copies of an irregular 3D shape into a box as possible, without any overlaps or pieces poking outside. The shapes can be rotated freely, so finding the best orientations is part of the puzzle.

The AI must handle 3D collision detection and spatial reasoning. Subpasses use increasingly awkward shapes that resist neat stacking. The baseline uses a simple grid placement without rotation optimisation.

 0.0000

Test 13: Longest Common Substring (C++) Score: 0.00 / 12
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the Longest Common Substring problem in C++.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY problem size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 2-3 short strings (10-50 chars each)
- **Medium**: 3-5 medium strings (100-500 chars each)
- **Large**: 5-10 long strings (1000-5000 chars each)
- **Extreme**: 100 very long strings (10MB+ each), massive search space

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N (number of strings)
Next N lines: one string per line

**Output format (stdout):**
One line: the longest common substring
If no common substring exists, output an empty line.

**Example:**
Input:
3
abcdef
xbcdey
zbcdew

Output:
bcde

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on number and length of strings
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 300 seconds even for very long strings
3. **Correctness**: Must find the actual longest common substring

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35730 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

Write complete, compilable C++ code with a main() function.
Include adaptive logic that chooses different strategies based on problem scale.

View chain of thought

Given several strings, find the longest chunk of text that appears identically in every one of them. For example, if the strings are “abcdef” and “xbcdey”, the answer is “bcde”.

Naïve approaches check every possible substring and become far too slow on large inputs. Subpasses increase the string lengths into the millions, so the AI must use efficient data structures or hashing. The baseline uses a simple library call that times out on the bigger cases.

 0.0000
[3 short strings (7 chars)] Invalid: Result must be string, got dict

Longest Common Substring - 3 short strings (7 chars)

View Code (1818 chars)
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>

// Function to find the longest common substring of two strings
std::string longestCommonSubstring(const std::string& s1, const std::string& s2) {
    int m = s1.length();
    int n = s2.length();

    // Create a 2D DP table to store the lengths of common suffixes
    // dp[i][j] stores the length of the longest common suffix of s1[0...i-1] and s2[0...j-1]
    std::vector<std::vector<int>> dp(m + 1, std::vector<int>(n + 1, 0));

    int maxLength = 0;      // Stores the length of the longest common substring
    int endIndex = 0;       // Stores the ending index of the longest common substring in s1

    // Fill the dp table
    for (int i = 1; i <= m; ++i) {
        for (int j = 1; j <= n; ++j) {
            if (s1[i - 1] == s2[j - 1]) {
                dp[i][j] = dp[i - 1][j - 1] + 1;
                if (dp[i][j] > maxLength) {
                    maxLength = dp[i][j];
                    endIndex = i - 1; // Current ending index in s1
                }
            } else {
                dp[i][j] = 0; // Reset if characters do not match
            }
        }
    }

    // If no common substring is found, return an empty string
    if (maxLength == 0) {
        return "";
    }

    // Extract the longest common substring using the maxLength and endIndex
    return s1.substr(endIndex - maxLength + 1, maxLength);
}

int main() {
    std::string s1, s2;

    // Get input strings from the user
    std::cout << "Enter the first string: ";
    std::cin >> s1;
    std::cout << "Enter the second string: ";
    std::cin >> s2;

    // Find and print the longest common substring
    std::string lcs = longestCommonSubstring(s1, s2);
    std::cout << "Longest Common Substring: " << lcs << std::endl;

    return 0;
}

Test 14: Minesweeper Solver (C#) Score: 0.00 / 10
Typical Prompt:
You are writing a Minesweeper solver in C# that must handle ANY board complexity from trivial to ludicrous scale.

- **Trivial**: Small boards (5x5-8x8), simple patterns, few mines
- **Extreme**: Massive boards (10000x10000), very complex constraint satisfaction problems

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: H W (height and width)
Next H lines: the board row strings (each W characters)

**Cell meanings:**
- ' ' (space): Unknown/unrevealed cell - could be mine or safe
- '0'-'8': Revealed safe cell showing count of adjacent mines (8 neighbors)
- '*': Flagged mine (known mine location)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: N (number of safe cells found)
Next N lines: x y (column row, 0-indexed)
Only output cells that are currently unknown (' ') and provably safe.
Do NOT guess - only return cells you can prove are safe.

**Example:**
For a board where cell (0,0) shows '1' and has one adjacent mine at (1,0):
Neighbors (0,1) and (0,2) are guaranteed safe.

**Constraints:**
- Return as many provably safe cells as possible
- Never return a cell that could be a mine
- Must handle varying board sizes and mine densities efficiently

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C# Compiler:**

Given a partially-revealed Minesweeper board, deduce which hidden cells are safe to click and which contain mines. Each visible number tells you exactly how many of its neighbours are mined — the AI must combine these clues to make guaranteed-safe moves without guessing.

Larger boards and denser mine layouts make the logic harder. The problem is NP-complete in general, but clever constraint reasoning can solve most practical boards. The baseline uses only the simplest single-cell deduction rules.

 0.0000
[8x8 board, 10 mines] Returned 4 unsafe cell(s) (mines!)

Minesweeper Solver - 8x8 board, 10 mines

View Code (8165 chars)
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;

public enum CellState
{
    Unknown,
    Revealed,
    Flagged
}

public class Cell
{
    public int Row { get; }
    public int Col { get; }
    public char Value { get; set; }
    public CellState State { get; set; }
    public int MineCount { get; set; } // Only relevant if State is Revealed and Value is '1'-'8'
    public bool InQueue { get; set; } // To track if the cell is currently in CellsToProcess queue

    // Counts of neighbors in different states. These are dynamically updated.
    public int UnknownNeighborsCount { get; set; }
    public int FlaggedNeighborsCount { get; set; }
    // SafeNeighborsCount is not directly used in the deductions as much as Unknown and Flagged.

    public Cell(int row, int col, char value, CellState state)
    {
        Row = row;
        Col = col;
        Value = value;
        State = state;
        MineCount = 0;
        InQueue = false; // Initialize InQueue to false
        if (state == CellState.Revealed && char.IsDigit(value))
        {
            MineCount = value - '0';
        }
    }

    public override string ToString()
    {
        return $"({Col},{Row}) {Value} {State}";
    }
}

public class Board
{
    public Cell[,] Cells { get; }
    public int Height { get; }
    public int Width { get; }
    public List<(int, int)> FoundSafeCells { get; }
    public Queue<Cell> CellsToProcess { get; } = new Queue<Cell>();

    public Board(int height, int width)
    {
        Height = height;
        Width = width;
        Cells = new Cell[Height, Width];
        FoundSafeCells = new List<(int, int)>();
    }

    public void ParseInput(string[] inputLines)
    {
        for (int r = 0; r < Height; r++)
        {
            string rowStr = inputLines[r + 1]; // +1 to skip H W line
            for (int c = 0; c < Width; c++)
            {
                char cellChar = rowStr[c];
                CellState state;
                if (cellChar == ' ')
                {
                    state = CellState.Unknown;
                }
                else if (cellChar == '*')
                {
                    state = CellState.Flagged; // Initial input '*' means a known mine, so it's flagged.
                }
                else // '0'-'8'
                {
                    state = CellState.Revealed;
                }
                Cells[r, c] = new Cell(r, c, cellChar, state);

                if (state == CellState.Revealed)
                {
                    CellsToProcess.Enqueue(Cells[r, c]);
                    Cells[r, c].InQueue = true;
                }
            }
        }
        
        // Initial calculation of neighbor counts for all cells
        for (int r = 0; r < Height; r++)
        {
            for (int c = 0; c < Width; c++)
            {
                UpdateNeighborCounts(Cells[r,c], initialSetup: true);
            }
        }
    }

    public IEnumerable<Cell> GetNeighbors(int row, int col)
    {
        for (int dr = -1; dr <= 1; dr++)
        {
            for (int dc = -1; dc <= 1; dc++)
            {
                if (dr == 0 && dc == 0) continue; // Skip self

                int nr = row + dr;
                int nc = col + dc;

                if (nr >= 0 && nr < Height && nc >= 0 && nc < Width)
                {
                    yield return Cells[nr, nc];
                }
            }
        }
    }

    // Helper to calculate neighbor counts for a cell.
    // initialSetup is true when called for all cells during ParseInput.
    // Otherwise, it's called when a neighbor's state changes.
    private void UpdateNeighborCounts(Cell cell, bool initialSetup = false)
    {
        int unknown = 0;
        int flagged = 0;

        foreach (var neighbor in GetNeighbors(cell.Row, cell.Col))
        {
            if (neighbor.State == CellState.Unknown)
            {
                unknown++;
            }
            else if (neighbor.State == CellState.Flagged)
            {
                flagged++;
            }
        }

        if (cell.UnknownNeighborsCount != unknown || cell.FlaggedNeighborsCount != flagged || initialSetup)
        {
            cell.UnknownNeighborsCount = unknown;
            cell.FlaggedNeighborsCount = flagged;

            // If a cell's neighbor counts change, it might lead to new deductions, so re-enqueue it.
            // Avoid adding already processed cells to the queue unless there's a new reason.
            if (!initialSetup && cell.State == CellState.Revealed && !cell.InQueue) 
            {
                CellsToProcess.Enqueue(cell);
                cell.InQueue = true;
            }
        }
    }

    public void ApplyDeductions(Cell cell)
    {
        if (cell.State != CellState.Revealed) return; // Only apply deductions from revealed numbered cells

        // Rule 1: If number of flagged neighbors equals cell's mine count, all remaining unknown neighbors are safe.
        if (cell.MineCount == cell.FlaggedNeighborsCount && cell.UnknownNeighborsCount > 0)
        {
            foreach (var neighbor in GetNeighbors(cell.Row, cell.Col))
            {
                if (neighbor.State == CellState.Unknown)
                {
                    MarkCellAsSafe(neighbor);
                }
            }
        }
        // Rule 2: If sum of flagged and unknown neighbors equals cell's mine count, all unknown neighbors are mines.
        else if (cell.MineCount == (cell.FlaggedNeighborsCount + cell.UnknownNeighborsCount) && cell.UnknownNeighborsCount > 0)
        {
            foreach (var neighbor in GetNeighbors(cell.Row, cell.Col))
            {
                if (neighbor.State == CellState.Unknown)
                {
                    MarkCellAsMine(neighbor);
                }
            }
        }
    }

    private void MarkCellAsSafe(Cell cell)
    {
        if (cell.State == CellState.Unknown)
        {
            cell.State = CellState.Revealed;
            cell.Value = '0'; // Temporarily set to '0', actual number is not known yet.
            FoundSafeCells.Add((cell.Col, cell.Row)); // Note: Output format is x y (column row)
            CellsToProcess.Enqueue(cell); // This newly revealed safe cell might have new deductions.
            cell.InQueue = true;

            // Update neighbor counts for all its neighbors.
            foreach (var neighborOfSafe in GetNeighbors(cell.Row, cell.Col))
            {
                UpdateNeighborCounts(neighborOfSafe);
            }
        }
    }

    private void MarkCellAsMine(Cell cell)
    {
        if (cell.State == CellState.Unknown)
        {
            cell.State = CellState.Flagged;
            cell.Value = '*';
            CellsToProcess.Enqueue(cell); // This newly flagged mine might have new deductions.
            cell.InQueue = true;

            // Update neighbor counts for all its neighbors.
            foreach (var neighborOfMine in GetNeighbors(cell.Row, cell.Col))
            {
                UpdateNeighborCounts(neighborOfMine);
            }
        }
    }

    public void Solve()
    {
        while (CellsToProcess.Any())
        {
            Cell currentCell = CellsToProcess.Dequeue();
            currentCell.InQueue = false; // Mark as no longer in queue
            ApplyDeductions(currentCell);
        }
    }
}

public class Program
{
    public static void Main(string[] args)
    {
        List<string> inputLines = new List<string>();
        string? line; // Declare as nullable string
        while ((line = Console.ReadLine()) != null && line != "")
        {
            inputLines.Add(line);
        }

        if (inputLines.Count == 0) return;

        string[] dimensions = inputLines[0].Split(' ');
        int height = int.Parse(dimensions[0]);
        int width = int.Parse(dimensions[1]);

        Board board = new Board(height, width);
        board.ParseInput(inputLines.ToArray());
        board.Solve();

        Console.WriteLine(board.FoundSafeCells.Count);
        foreach (var coord in board.FoundSafeCells)
        {
            Console.WriteLine($"{coord.Item1} {coord.Item2}");
        }
    }
}
